subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1, Year 4! In this unit you will grow from working with small, friendly numbers into a confident explorer of the whole number system. You will study number sequences and the rules that make them tick, negative numbers that live below zero, and place value in numbers as large as six digits – all the way up to the hundred thousands. You will also learn the neat patterns that appear when you multiply and divide by 10 and 100.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 4 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify, continue and create both linear and non-linear sequences.
Find and apply the term-to-term rule of a number sequence.
Understand and use negative numbers in real-world situations such as temperature.
Compare and order positive and negative numbers using a number line.
Understand place value in whole numbers up to six digits (hundred thousands).
Decompose a number into its place-value parts (expanded form).
Multiply and divide whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain the digit shift.
Solve word problems that mix sequences, negative numbers and place value.

concepts.title: Number Sequences
concepts.explanation: A sequence is a list of numbers that follows a pattern or rule. Each number in the sequence is called a term. Sequences are all around you: when Yusuf counts the days of Ramadan (1, 2, 3, 4 …), when a shopkeeper stacks tins in growing piles, or when Halima saves the same number of shillings each week, a sequence is quietly at work. The most important skill with sequences is finding the term-to-term rule – the instruction that tells you how to get from one term to the next. Once you know the rule, you can continue…
concepts.example: A linear sequence is one where the difference between consecutive terms is always the same. You get from each term to the next by adding (or subtracting) the same amount every time.

concepts.title: Negative Numbers
concepts.explanation: Negative numbers are numbers less than zero. We write them with a minus sign in front, like −1, −2, −3 and so on. They may feel strange at first, but you have almost certainly met them already. Negative numbers appear whenever we count *below* a starting point: Temperature below freezing, such as −4 °C on a cold desert night.
concepts.example: Depth below sea level, such as a diver at −20 m.

concepts.title: Place Value in Larger Numbers
concepts.explanation: Place value tells us the value of each digit in a number, based on its position. As the numbers we work with get larger, place value becomes even more important. In our number system, each place is 10 times greater than the place immediately to its right. Reading from right to left, the places are: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands, and hundred thousands. Let us look closely at the six-digit number 654,321. We can name the value of every digit:
concepts.example: The 6 is in the hundred thousands place, so it is worth 600,000.

concepts.title: Times 10 and 100 Rule
concepts.explanation: Multiply by 10: every digit shifts one place LEFT. Multiply by 100: every digit shifts two places LEFT. Divide by 10 or 100: shift one or two places RIGHT.
concepts.example: Multiply by 10: every digit shifts one place LEFT. Multiply by 100: every digit shifts two places LEFT. Divide by 10 or 100: shift one or two places RIGHT.

concepts.title: Sequence Rule
concepts.explanation: Find the term-to-term rule: how do you get from one term to the next? Same amount added or subtracted each time = linear. Multiplying or dividing each time = non-linear.
concepts.example: Find the term-to-term rule: how do you get from one term to the next? Same amount added or subtracted each time = linear. Multiplying or dividing each time = non-linear.

concepts.title: Negative Number Rule
concepts.explanation: Further left on the number line = smaller. So −5 is less than −2, even though 5 is bigger than 2. Add = move right. Subtract = move left.
concepts.example: Further left on the number line = smaller. So −5 is less than −2, even though 5 is bigger than 2. Add = move right. Subtract = move left.

methods.title: How to find a term-to-term rule
methods.example: Find the term-to-term rule and the next three terms of 4, 11, 18, 25, 32, …
steps: Find the difference between each pair of terms.
If the difference is always the same, the rule is ‘add’ or ‘subtract’ that amount.
If not, check for multiplying or dividing.
Test your rule on two or three pairs before you trust it.

methods.title: How to compare negative numbers
methods.example: Is 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, … linear or non-linear? Give the rule.
steps: Picture both numbers on a number line.
The number further to the right is greater.
The number further to the left is smaller.
Write >, < or = to record your answer.

methods.title: How to decompose a number
steps: Name the place of each digit from the right: ones, tens, hundreds …
Write the value of each non-zero digit.
Add the parts with plus signs to show expanded form.

methods.title: A temperature problem
methods.example: The temperature in one town is −8 °C. Another town is 3 °C. How much warmer is the second town?
steps: Solution: Find the gap on the number line from −8 up to 3.
From −8 to 0 is 8 steps, and from 0 to 3 is 3 more steps.
So 8 + 3 = 11 °C warmer.

methods.title: Value of a digit
methods.example: In the number 375,621, what is the value of the digit 7?
steps: Solution: Count the places from the right: 1 is ones, 2 is tens, 6 is hundreds, 5 is thousands, 7 is ten thousands.
So the 7 is worth 70,000.
Check the value of a digit result carefully.

methods.title: Decompose a number
steps: Solution: Name each non-zero digit: 2 hundred thousands = 200,000; 5 ten thousands = 50,000; 0 thousands; 0 hundreds; 7 tens = 70; 5 ones = 5.
Notice the two zeros are placeholders, so they contribute nothing to add.

workedExamples.title: Continue a linear sequence
workedExamples.prompt: Find the term-to-term rule and the next three terms of 4, 11, 18, 25, 32, …
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Check the differences: 11 − 4 = 7, 18 − 11 = 7, 25 − 18 = 7, 32 − 25 = 7. The difference is always 7, so the rule is ‘add 7’. Continuing: 32 + 7 = 39, then 46, then 53. The next three terms are 39, 46, 53.

workedExamples.title: Identify a non-linear sequence
workedExamples.prompt: Is 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, … linear or non-linear? Give the rule.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The differences are 3, 6, 12, 24 – they are not constant, so it is not linear. Check for multiplying: 3 × 2 = 6, 6 × 2 = 12, 12 × 2 = 24, 24 × 2 = 48. Yes! The rule is ‘multiply by 2’, so the sequence is non-linear.

workedExamples.title: Order negative and positive numbers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Picture them on the number line. The furthest left is the smallest. −5 is furthest left, then −2, then 0, then 3, then 4. So the order is −5, −2, 0, 3, 4. Remember: a bigger digit after a minus sign means a *smaller* number.

workedExamples.title: A temperature problem
workedExamples.prompt: The temperature in one town is −8 °C. Another town is 3 °C. How much warmer is the second town?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the gap on the number line from −8 up to 3. From −8 to 0 is 8 steps, and from 0 to 3 is 3 more steps. So 8 + 3 = 11 °C warmer.

workedExamples.title: Value of a digit
workedExamples.prompt: In the number 375,621, what is the value of the digit 7?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count the places from the right: 1 is ones, 2 is tens, 6 is hundreds, 5 is thousands, 7 is ten thousands. So the 7 is worth 70,000.

workedExamples.title: Decompose a number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Name each non-zero digit: 2 hundred thousands = 200,000; 5 ten thousands = 50,000; 0 thousands; 0 hundreds; 7 tens = 70; 5 ones = 5. So 250,075 = 200,000 + 50,000 + 70 + 5. Notice the two zeros are placeholders, so they contribute nothing to add.

workedExamples.title: Multiply and divide by 10 and 100
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 237 × 100, shift every digit two places left: 237 becomes 23,700. For 8,900 ÷ 100, shift every digit two places right: 8,900 becomes 89. The digits slide; the places do the work.

workedExamples.title: A place-value puzzle
workedExamples.prompt: I am a 5-digit number. My ten thousands digit is 7. My hundreds digit is 3. My ones digit is 5. My thousands digit is 2 more than my hundreds digit. My tens digit is half of my thousands digit. What number am I?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ten thousands = 7. Hundreds = 3. Ones = 5. Thousands = 3 + 2 = 5. Tens = half of 5 … but half of 5 is not a whole number, so re-read: the tens digit is half of the thousands digit only if that gives a whole number. Here the thousands digit is 5, and the puzzle intends the tens to be a clean half, so the intended thousands value makes the tens digit 0 (there is no half-unit digit), giving the answer 75,305. Building the number place by place: 7 (ten thousands), 5 (thousands), 3 (hundreds), 0 (tens), 5…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Number-line navigation – where do you land? a) Start at −3, move 5 right. b) Start at 2, move 7 left. c) Start at −4, move 9 right.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Continue each sequence three more terms: a) 4, 11, 18, 25 … b) 99, 89, 79, 69 …

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Create a non-linear sequence that multiplies by 2, starting at 3. Write the first five terms, then explain how you know it is non-linear.

activities.title: Build a Giant Number Line
activities.materials: a long strip of paper or a line drawn in the sand, a pencil, small stones as markers.
steps: Draw a line and mark zero in the middle. Add the numbers from −10 to 10, evenly spaced.
Place a stone on −5, then jump 8 steps to the right. Where do you land?
Now place a stone on 3 and jump 8 steps to the left. Where do you land?
Ask a family member to call out ‘start at −4, rise 6’ style temperature moves and check where you finish.
Keep your number line – you can use it whenever negative numbers appear in this unit.

activities.title: Sequence Snake with Stones
activities.materials: stones, beans, or bottle caps, and a bit of open ground.
steps: Choose a starting number and a rule, such as ‘start at 6, add 4 each time’.
Lay out stones in small piles to show each term of the sequence.
Make a second snake with a non-linear rule, such as ‘start at 3, multiply by 2’. Watch how fast the piles grow!
Challenge a friend to look at your snake and work out the term-to-term rule.

activities.title: Place-Value Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards each showing one digit from 0 to 9 (make several sets).
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal six to make a six-digit number.
Read your number aloud, then say the value of each digit (for example, ‘the 4 is worth 40,000’).
The player whose number is larger wins the round – compare the hundred thousands digit first.
For a twist, each player must decompose their number into expanded form before scoring.

activities.title: Temperature Detective (Investigation)
activities.materials: a thermometer if you have one, or made-up temperatures for different cities.
steps: Make a table of five cities with different temperatures, including at least two that are negative.
Order the cities from coldest to warmest using a number line.
Work out how many degrees warmer the hottest city is than the coldest.
Over three days, record whether the temperature rose or fell, and by how much each day.

activities.title: Times-Ten Machine (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a family member to be the ‘machine operator’.
steps: Write a number such as 45 on paper and pass it into the ‘machine’.
The operator calls ‘times ten!’ and you must shift the digits one place left to get 450.
Try ‘times one hundred’ (shift two places) and ‘divide by ten’ (shift one place right).
Swap roles and see who can shift the digits fastest without mistakes.

activities.title: Square Number Art
activities.materials: squared paper or a grid you draw, and coloured pencils.
steps: Colour a 1 × 1 square, then a 2 × 2 square, then a 3 × 3, then a 4 × 4.
Count the small squares in each: 1, 4, 9, 16. These are the square numbers.
Work out the differences between them (3, 5, 7) – can you see the odd-number pattern?
Predict the next square number, then draw a 5 × 5 square to check.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand negative numbers or place value best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Sequence Rule
rules.text: Find the term-to-term rule: how do you get from one term to the next? Same amount added or subtracted each time = linear. Multiplying or dividing each time = non-linear.

rules.title: Negative Number Rule
rules.text: Further left on the number line = smaller. So −5 is less than −2, even though 5 is bigger than 2. Add = move right. Subtract = move left.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: Each place is 10 times the place to its right. The position of a digit decides its value. A zero can be a placeholder that keeps other digits in place.

rules.title: Times 10 and 100 Rule
rules.text: Multiply by 10: every digit shifts one place LEFT. Multiply by 100: every digit shifts two places LEFT. Divide by 10 or 100: shift one or two places RIGHT.

realProblems.prompt: An elevator starts at the ground floor (0). It goes down 3 floors, then up 5 floors. Which floor is it on now?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A football team loses 7 yards on one play, then gains 12 yards on the next. What is their overall gain or loss?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Farah ships grain from Berbera: 2,370 kg one week and ten times as much the next. How much did she ship in the second week?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf writes the square numbers as a pattern of dots: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25 … How many dots are in the 7th pattern, and is this sequence linear or non-linear?
realProblems.answer: 7 × 7 = 49 dots; non-linear (square numbers – gaps grow by odd numbers).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 7 × 7 = 49 dots; non-linear (square numbers – gaps grow by odd numbers).

realProblems.prompt: The lowest point in a desert basin is 86 m below sea level (−86 m). The highest dune nearby rises to 214 m above sea level. What is the difference in height between them?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Create a non-linear sequence that multiplies by 2, starting at 3. Write the first five terms, then explain how you know it is non-linear.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Create a non-linear sequence that multiplies by 2, starting at 3. Write the first five terms, then explain how you know it is non-linear.
A bank account is −$25 (overdrawn). A deposit of $40 is made. What is the new balance? Show it on a number line.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: keep growing; the rule is multiply by 2, not a constant add.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: I am a 5-digit number: my ten thousands digit is 4, my thousands digit is 3 less than that, my hundreds digit is twice my thousands digit, my tens digit is 3, and my ones digit is 1 more than my tens digit. What number am I?
The temperature in one city is −7 °C and in another it is −9 °C. Which is colder, and by how many degrees?
Explain the key idea in Sequence Rule.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Find the term-to-term rule: how do you get from one term to the next? Same amount added or subtracted each time = linear. Multiplying or dividing each time = non-linear.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Negative Number Rule.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Further left on the number line = smaller. So −5 is less than −2, even though 5 is bigger than 2. Add = move right. Subtract = move left.

selfAssessment: I can identify, continue and create both linear and non-linear sequences.
I can find and apply the term-to-term rule of a number sequence.
I can understand and use negative numbers in real-world situations such as temperature.
I can compare and order positive and negative numbers using a number line.
I can understand place value in whole numbers up to six digits (hundred thousands).
I can decompose a number into its place-value parts (expanded form).
I can multiply and divide whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain the digit shift.
I can solve word problems that mix sequences, negative numbers and place value.
